description: Typed sync engine and coordination API for apps where humans, server code, and AI agents edit the same data. Current API (0.9.x): single-options-object model verbs and disposable claims.
rules: Use the 0.9.x API only. Model writes take ONE options object: create({ data }), update({ id, data }), delete({ id }), retrieve({ id }). The positional forms update(id, data) / create(data, opts) / delete(id) are REMOVED — never generate them.

Coordinate concurrent writers with disposable claims: `await using claim = await ablo.<model>.claim({ id })`, read the fresh row from `claim.data`, then write, and the claim auto-releases at scope end. The callback form claim(id, cb) and claim('id', async (row) => {}) are REMOVED.

Reads come in two flavors: synchronous reactive local reads get(id) / getAll({ where }) (use in render and with useAblo selectors), and async server reads retrieve({ id }) / list({ where }). There is NO load() method.

The default integration owns its own database. Keep your rows in your own Postgres and expose a Data Source endpoint that hands Ablo an adapter built from your ORM client and the schema — `drizzleDataSource(db, schema)` (preferred) or `prismaDataSource(prisma, schema)` — via `dataSourceNext({ schema, apiKey, adapter })` with `export const runtime = 'nodejs'`. Your `DATABASE_URL` lives in your app and is NEVER passed to `Ablo(...)`; the Ablo client takes only `apiKey` (server) or `authEndpoint` (browser). Run `npx ablo migrate` to provision the synced-model tables in your DB, then `npx ablo push`.

In React, AbloProvider takes only a `client` prop (plus userId/fallback/onError): build `const ablo = Ablo({ schema, authEndpoint: '/api/ablo-session' })` then `<AbloProvider client={ablo}>`. withSync is REMOVED — wrap components with `observer` from mobx-react-lite, or prefer the useAblo selector.

After constructing the client, `await ablo.ready()` once before the first read or write — it drives the initial sync/bootstrap and is required for user-kind clients. `await ablo.dispose()` tears the client down. Both are real lifecycle methods.

After creating or changing ablo/schema.ts you MUST run `npx ablo push` (or keep `npx ablo dev` running). The server keeps its own copy of the schema; writing to a model it has not seen fails with server_execute_unknown_model.
Start a new integration with `npx ablo init --yes` — it scaffolds the schema, client, provider, session route, and Data Source endpoint on the current API. Do not hand-write the client from memory.
